mechanism: Compute a matrix of Pearson correlations across measured psychological scales related to mating preferences, separately for each group (heterosexual men, heterosexual women, homosexual men, homosexual women). Examine the structure of intercorrelations to assess whether the pattern of relationships among scales is similar across groups despite potential differences in absolute scale values. Test hypotheses about sex differences predicted from sexual selection theory by comparing mean scale scores across gender and sexual orientation groups, using standard statistical tests (t-tests or analysis of variance). Assess whether the correlation/factor structure is invariant across groups and whether scale values follow predicted theoretical patterns.

Formulate a probabilistic model where an agent observes a small set of positive examples of a novel object category and must infer the extension of the category via Bayesian inference. Specify a prior distribution P(hypothesis) over plausible word meanings, a likelihood P(observed examples | hypothesis) that defines how consistent observed examples are with each candidate hypothesis, and a data-likelihood term capturing the probability of observing the given set of examples under each hypothesis. Apply Bayes' rule to compute the posterior distribution P(hypothesis | observed examples) by combining prior and likelihood. The posterior defines predicted category membership for novel test objects. Model parameters and structure are fit to human behavioral judgments. The mechanism integrates domain knowledge (prior constraints on plausible word meanings) with statistical structure of observed examples to perform rational inductive generalization.

Administer a sequential priming paradigm where a short-exposure prime face (stimulus A) is presented, followed by a target word or image requiring rapid categorization (stimulus B, classified as threatening or nonthreatening). Record reaction times and error rates for target categorization. Apply process dissociation procedure: decompose observed performance into automatic (unintentional) and controlled (intentional) components via a formal latent-variable model that estimates two independent processes from the joint distribution of accuracy and latency. Model the automatic component as independent of conscious control and the controlled component as an additional decision process. Use maximum-likelihood estimation or similar to recover parameter estimates for automatic and controlled effect magnitudes separately.

Collect repeated measurements of explanatory variables (optimism, control, self-esteem, coping strategies) and outcome variables (college adjustment, mood, grades) at multiple time points (initial assessment and 2-year follow-up). Fit a system of regression equations where adjustment outcome is modeled as a function of baseline individual-difference predictors, with coping strategies as intermediate mediators. Formally test indirect effects (paths through mediators) via product-of-coefficients method or similar. Estimate standardized path coefficients and assess mediation by testing whether the direct effect of predictors is reduced or eliminated when mediating variables enter the model. Fit alternative causal model structures and compare fit via chi-square or information criteria to select the most plausible causal ordering.

Measure affect and coping strategy adoption in a sample at two time points separated by 5 weeks. Construct a set of lagged regression equations where positive affect at time 2 is regressed on positive affect at time 1 (autoregressive term) plus broad-minded coping at time 1 (cross-lagged predictor), and vice versa (broad-minded coping at time 2 regressed on coping at time 1 plus prior affect). Estimate regression coefficients for cross-lagged paths and test significance. Perform mediational analysis by decomposing total effects into direct effects and indirect effects transmitted through serial mediation (affect affects coping which feeds back to affect). Test reciprocal causation by examining whether both cross-lagged paths are significant and by comparing fit of reciprocal model against unidirectional alternatives.

Apply factor analysis to an N by P data matrix where N is the number of subjects and P is the number of items or scales measuring spiritual constructs. Compute the sample correlation or covariance matrix. Estimate common factors via principal factor method or maximum likelihood. Extract latent factors by solving the eigenvalue equation to identify dimensions underlying manifest spirituality measures. Rotate factors (orthogonal or oblique rotation) to enhance interpretability and obtain factor loadings (regression coefficients of items on factors). Compute factor scores for each subject. In Study 2, construct a new measurement instrument (98-item test) by selecting items with high loadings on the identified factors. Assess reliability via internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha for each scale) and validity via correlations with external variables (Five Factor Model traits).

Administer three alternative instruments measuring the Big Five personality dimensions to subjects in two independent samples. Record behavioral criterion variables (alcohol consumption, academic performance, etc.) for each subject. Compute Pearson correlations or fit regression models predicting each behavioral criterion from the five personality dimensions in sample 1. Repeat the same regression fits in sample 2. Compare regression coefficients and their significance across the two samples and across the three personality instruments. Assess consistency (replicability) of prediction patterns and effect sizes. Test the hypothesis that personality dimensions explain a stable, generalizable portion of criterion variance.

Collect self-rated and partner-rated measures of personality traits (Big Five and trait affect) from both members of each romantic couple. Construct four matrices: target's self-ratings, partner's ratings of target, partner's self-ratings, and target's ratings of partner. Measure relationship satisfaction for each person. Fit regression models predicting target's satisfaction from each of the four rating matrices (explanatory variables are personality scores, outcome is satisfaction). Compute correlation matrices and estimate regression coefficients. Compare effect sizes and significance of personality predictors across the four models. Assess variance explained (R-squared) by personality traits jointly and separately for trait affect, conscientiousness, agreeableness, extraversion.
